text.title: Migration Paths
text.summary: Two migration directions: from traditional monolith to modular monolith (add boundaries), and from modular monolith to microservices via Strangler Fig (extract with data). Data extraction must accompany code extraction — extracting code while leaving a shared database creates a distributed monolith.

rule.statement: Modularize incrementally: identify domains, establish public APIs, separate schemas, add boundary enforcement — one module at a time, never all at once.
rule.rationale: Big-bang refactors of existing monoliths fail. Incremental extraction validates assumptions and keeps the system deployable throughout.

list.item: 1. Identify business capabilities and group into candidate modules
2. Extract shared infrastructure to a minimal shared/ package (DTOs, base exceptions, event bus)
3. Create module package directories with internal/ for private code
4. Define public API per module (api.py / api.go / Api.java)
5. Separate database schemas (CREATE SCHEMA per module)
6. Update all callers to use public APIs instead of direct imports
7. Add boundary enforcement linter and wire into CI

section.title: Strangler Fig: Modular Monolith to Microservices

rule.statement: Extract one module at a time using Strangler Fig. A module is ready to extract only when it has: a clear public API, its own database schema, and event-based (or clearly bounded synchronous) communication. Extract the database with the service or do not extract at all.
rule.rationale: The most common microservices extraction failure is extracting the code while leaving the shared database. This creates a distributed monolith: network overhead and operational complexity without any of the isolation benefits.

list.item: 1. Verify: module has clean API boundary, owns its schema, no cross-schema joins
2. Copy module code to a new deployable repository
3. Add an in-monolith facade that routes calls to either old or new service
4. Replace in-process calls with HTTP/gRPC at the facade boundary
5. Replace in-memory events with message queue (Kafka, RabbitMQ)
6. Extract and migrate the module's database schema to its own instance
7. Remove the module from the monolith once the service is proven in production

antipattern.description: Extracting service code while keeping tables in the shared monolith database
antipattern.reason: Creates tight runtime coupling via the database; the extracted service cannot be deployed independently and schema changes still block the monolith
